figures.caption: Figure 2. Dopamine (DA) increases theta relative power in PFC and HPC in a dose-dependent manner. 217 (A-B) Log-log power spectra (10-min post-injection vs. baseline) for PFC (top) and HPC (bottom) after DA 218

Figure 4. DA reorganizes PFC single-unit activity and enhances spike-LFP theta phase locking. (A) DA 500 264 nmol reduces PFC neuronal firing rates. Spike raster (top) displays spikes from representative neurons. 265 Population z-scored firing-rate histogram shows the average decrease in neuronal activity after DA 266 injection (nneurons=131, nrats=6). The vertical blue shaded bar represents the drug injection window. The 267 slope graph (right) shows per-unit paired firing-rate changes; units with decreasing firing rate were 268 highlighted in red. (B) Preferred theta phase (left) and phase locking values (right) before vs. after DA. DA 269 500 nmol induces spike-LFP theta synchrony and phase shift. The slope graph (right) shows per-unit paired 270 phase locking values changes; units with increasing phase locking values were highlighted in red. (C-D) 271 Saline condition shows no significant change (nneurons=70, nrats=6). # p<0.05 and ## p<0.01 for paired two-272 tailed t-tests. 273

Figure 3. DA induces HPC-PFC theta phase synchrony and coherence in a dose-dependent manner. (A-239 B) Spectral coherence (top) and phase synchrony by debiased weighted phase-lag index (dwPLI; bottom) 240 averaged over 10-min post-injection vs. baseline after DA 100 nmol (A) and 500 nmol (B). DA 500 nmol 241 induces theta coherence and phase synchrony (nrats=13), while DA 100 nmol showed weaker increases 242 (nrats=8); paired two-tailed t-tests. Error bars represent SEM. (C-D) HPC-PFC coherograms for each dose. 243 The vertical bar represents the 2-minute injection period. (E) Dose-response of change in theta synchrony. 244 DA 500 nmol shows higher theta coherence and phase synchrony modulation compared to DA 100 nmol 245 and saline. Points and error bars are linear mixed model (LMM) estimated marginal means ± SEM. # 246 p<0.05 and ## p<0.01 for t-tests statistics; ** p<0.05 for LMM Holm-adjusted post hoc pairwise 247 comparison. 248

Figure 6. Apomorphine (i.p.) induces a dose-dependent modulation of HPC-PFC theta activity. (A) PFC 332 theta/delta relative power ratio over time for 0.75, 1.5, and 3 mg/kg (normalized to baseline). Increasing 333 the dose leads to an increase in theta relative power predominance over delta. The analysis window is 30-334 40 min post-dose to account for delays in the onset of effect following i.p. administration. Shaded areas 335 around the lines represent SEM. (B) Dose-response of theta relative power change (post-injection - 336 baseline). The magnitude of the change in relative power resembles the pattern shown by the theta/delta 337 ratio. However, LMM analysis shows that the effect is more pronounced in the PFC. (C) Theta coherence 338 (top row) and phase synchrony (bottom row) at each dose for post-injection vs. baseline, illustrating 339 nonsignificant dose-linked shifts in synchrony measures when compared to baseline. Paired two-tailed t-340 tests. Error bars represent SEM. (D) Dose-response of theta synchrony (coherence, dwPLI). Relative to the 341 saline injection, APO induces a strong positive modulation of theta phase synchrony while reducing theta 342

Figure 5. Selective D1 (SKF-38393) or D2 (quinpirole) receptor agonism does not reproduce DA-induced 293 HPC-PFC theta modulation. (A) HPC-PFC coherence (top) and phase synchrony (dwPLI; bottom) averaged 294 over 10-min post-injection vs. baseline, with theta bar plots (right; error bars represent SEM), for SKF 10 295 μg (nrats=7). (B) Dose-response of changes in theta relative power (left) and synchrony (right; coherence, 296 dwPLI) across saline and both SKF doses (1 μg and 10 μg). (C) Coherence and phase synchrony for QUIN 297 10 μg with corresponding bar plots (nrats=6). Neither agonist produced consistent changes in theta power 298 or synchrony; paired two-tailed t-tests. (D) Dose-response of power and synchrony across saline, and both 299 QUIN doses (1 μg and 10 μg). For dose-response panels, points and error bars are linear mixed models 300 (LMM) estimated marginal means ± SEM. * p<0.05 for LMM main effect. 301 Apomorphine exhibits a linear modulation of HPC-PFC oscillatory dynamics. 302 To further investigate whether DA-induced effects can be reproduced by 303 concurrent D1/D2 activation, we administered three different doses of the non-selective 304 DA receptor agonist apomorphine (APO; 0.75, 1.5, and 3 mg/kg; i.p.). Due to APO 305

Figure 1. Experimental design and analytical workflow. (A) Surgical targeting and post hoc histology. Left: 172 schematic showing hippocampal (HPC) and medial prefrontal cortex (PFC) recording locations from forty-173 two recordings, and a single intracerebroventricular (i.c.v.) cannula for drug injection. Right, top-to-174 bottom: representative coronal sections showing a 32-channel silicon probe spanning PFC (left 175 hemisphere), a cannula tract into the right lateral ventricle for drug infusion, and an electrolytic lesion in 176 CA1 (left hemisphere) marking tungsten-wire electrode placement for recordings. (B) Recording timeline. 177 Animals underwent a 30-60 min baseline, followed by post-injection epochs (saline, then one or two drug 178 doses; order counterbalanced). (C) The 6-10 Hz range was selected for LFP power and HPC-PFC 179 connectivity analyses since it encompassed the peak of the post-minus-pre difference in phase synchrony 180 (dwPLI). As dwPLI selectively captures non-zero phase-lag interactions, this approach minimizes the 181 contribution of volume-conducted signals. (D) Analysis pipeline. Top: representative raw PFC LFP trace 182 before and after drug infusion. Middle: spectral synchrony (power spectral density and magnitude-183 squared coherence) and phase-synchrony (debiased weighted phase-lag index; dwPLI) measures 184 computed from HPC-PFC LFPs. Bottom: spike sorting followed by spike-LFP synchrony (phase locking) 185 analysis. 186
